speaker: interviewee
text: --by myself, and I lived at my Uncle Artur's house, who was a dentist. He had a very nice apartment, and he had his dentist office in the apartment. He had two rooms set aside. One was the waiting room, and one was the room you went in when he dealt with people. And he had-- in those days, Vienna, to me, was a big shock. I had, all my life, my young life, heard about wonderful Vienna. My mother played the beautiful songs from Vienna. Vienna, city of my dreams, and the Lehar songs, I knew all of these things. And I heard so much about Vienna and the beautiful city. It was a total shock when I got there, because I thought it was backwards. I came from a modern industrial town-- city. And Vienna, for all it's splendor-- and it was beautiful-- it was backwards to me. Because my uncle, of course, had a large apartment. And he had a bathroom, but most people didn't have a bathroom. They had to share a toilet with two other tenants on the same floor, or something like that. And a lot of people didn't have running water in the apartment that I found out. I was totally shocked. I had never heard of such a thing. They also had to share one spigot of a basin outside, like we once-- we finally had an apartment like that. Where you went outside, and you had one of these great big pails that you used to fill with water and take it inside so you had for cooking and washing and stuff like that. But there was no running water. Now, he had running water. Cold water. And he had a toilet, one of these old-fashioned things with one of those big tanks, and you had to pull the string. And the whole house knew you went to the toilet because it made a lot of noise. But that was-- I mean, this was supposed to be only for rich people. Ordinary middle class people, even though they were not bad off-- a lot of them lived in nice apartments-- they didn't have that. And when I found that out, I thought it was-- And then also the people that I met. Now, I was brought up very formal because, in those days, everybody was formal. But Vienna, I thought, was ridiculous. I mean, every young man or older man, if you met a lady, he used to say, I'll kiss your hand, madam. [GERMAN] That means, I kiss your hand. And he would actually kiss her hand. It was so formal that, let me explain something. Like, if you're-- if a woman was married to a doctor, she was [GERMAN]. If her husband was a general, she was [GERMAN]. I mean, they were all with the titles. And some of them had ridiculously long titles that were ridiculous. They were like in the 18th century, I thought. They weren't living in the modern world. They were so steeped in all of these things. I couldn't understand it. And I didn't like it. But that's the way it was in Vienna in those days.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the city like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The city was beautiful. My cousin took me around. And he took me to the Burgtheater and the other theaters, and so on and so forth. It was beautiful. But when I met some of the people, I thought they were not as educated as the ones in Germany. They weren't. And also, I came from a-- from a city that was mostly Lutheran into a Catholic city. And I had never known how influential that was. It was totally Catholic.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the influence? Describe it.

speaker: interviewee
text: The influence was everybody was Catholic. And if you weren't Catholic, you were an outsider. There were a lot of Jews there, but they had their own little ghetto and their own little things. They did not intermix as much as in Germany. And in Germany, I didn't know the difference. If I had kids, I didn't ask them whether they were Jewish. But in Austria, everybody asked you right away. There was this separation of the Jews and the non-Jews. Immediately, you found out you were not. So the summer wasn't too bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1933. I went there in-- you know, in the summer, because there's no school in the summer. So I sort of could acclimatize myself a little to that. And I lived in my Uncle Artur's house. And my cousin was very nice to me in the beginning. He took me around and he took me to the theater. And my uncle-- and, you know, it was wonderful in one respect. You took a subway a half an hour, and you were in the Vienna Woods. And in the winter, they used to go skiing there. You just took the subway for half an hour, and you could go skiing. They were both skiers. I was not. And I wanted to learn. My father didn't allow it. He says, I don't need you to break your leg. He didn't trust me, apparently. To this day, I'm sorry, I didn't learn how to ski. It would have been so easy. But I was able to take a sled. And I got the heavy shoes and the things. And we went. I went with them to the Vienna Woods. It was really wonderful. In a half hour, you were in the woods and walk around. It was just beautiful. I mean, the city is beautiful. But I thought it was backwards. I really did, at that time, and I was only 12. I thought it was a backwards city. And then I wanted to know how did people take baths. And then I found out about the public bath. Everybody went to the public bath. Of course, men and women separated. And there they had showers and bidets. That was wonderful.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHING] And how often?

speaker: interviewee
text: Once a week. You could go as much as you wanted to, I guess. But I don't even know whether you had to pay. I don't remember that. I just know once a week, you went to a public bath, because my uncle had a toilet, but he didn't have a bathroom. There was no bathtub. And I was used to having a bathtub and a bathroom. And the other thing-- oh, I forgot to tell you that, in Germany, I had a dog, a gray terrier. And my mother had promised to bring him when they came, but they didn't. She said he was quite old. He was, I think, 14, 15. And she said she gave him to a family who took good care, but I know in my heart that they put him to sleep. And I cried bitter tears. That was my Lux. His name was Lux, L-U-X. And that was a terrible shock. My parents came in the fall. And we had a nice apartment. We even had a bathroom, and-- with one of those tanks that you pulled on. It was a very nice apartment. I guess my father still had some money left. And we lived in a nice section, and I went to public school. I was told that now that my brother had to go to gymnasium, there wasn't enough money for both of us. And it was more important that a boy goes to school because, eventually, he has to support a family, and I wouldn't. I cried bitter tears because I thought that was unfair. And I wanted to go to the lyceum, gymnasium, whatever they had, and they sent me to a public school. That was the most boring time I had. Everything I already had knew. I was so far ahead, especially in French. I could speak French, and they were first learning little words here and there. And I wasn't getting along with the kids, either. They knew I was a Jewish refugee. Right away, they knew where you came from. You were Jewish. That's why you are here. And they kicked you out of Germany. And so not only this, but I knew everything. They knew I didn't have to read anything. And I could just give the lessons when I was called upon. And they knew I didn't study because I knew it all. I was bored. I was just bored stiff. The only thing that I was interested in that I didn't know I learned, I think it was shorthand-- German shorthand. That was interesting. It was something I didn't know. But the rest of it, I could have gone to sleep and I would have known everything there was to know. So I wasn't very popular. But we also had one incident I would like to tell you. Because we had a class in sewing and embroidery that every girl had to take that. It was a must. Cooking, no, but embroidery. And I was not very good at this. We all had to have something that we made. It was usually a nightgown, wear a square nightgown, but it was embroidered with something. And I had very much trouble with that. And I had a terrible teacher. She hated me. I don't know why. She just hated me. And she always picked on me and said I didn't finish that and I didn't finish that. And at one point, she made an anti-Semitic remark to me. She said-- because I said, well, you're helping these people, but you never help me. So she says, well, I don't want to help a lousy Jew or something. She said something very anti-Semitic.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was very shy in those days and scared to death of everybody, but she just hit the wrong button. I took my whole basket and I threw it at her. And it hit the wall. And she said, you're going to go down to the director for that. But you know something? She didn't. She would have had to explain what she said to me, and, apparently, she didn't want to do that.

speaker: interviewee
text: But she took the stuff, and the next day when I came back, she gave it to me and she had finished it. [LAUGHING] We never talked to it, but she never went to the director. She didn't complain about me. But from then on, she was a little bit nicer, but she didn't like me. To her, I was a Jewish refugee. And, also, I was-- I was just not popular with the girls and not popular with teachers. I was bored and I was just-- couldn't wait to get out of that school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened next? Well, my parents came. And my father tried to make a living. He kept on saying, we can't stay here. Hitler is going to come here. We have to-- There, I met all the relatives. I met my grandmothers. My grandma was the only one who was happy. She had all of her brothers and sisters there. She had an older brother who was also a dentist, had been a dentist for 30 years or something. He lived in a different section. He never married. He lived with a housekeeper, and she did all the things for him. She cooked. And those were the things that they did in those days. Now, she could have easily lived with him. He had a large apartment. But she didn't. And then, she had a sister. She had two sisters. One of them died. I remember. Shari, I think it was. The other sister, I forgot her name. Also, they had no children. They were also quite old, in their 60s. Her husband, I think, was an accountant. He had been working-- he's the only man I knew-- Jewish man I knew who had a job. Most of the men either had-- were in business or they had a profession. They were either professors or teachers or, you know, dentists or something or the other. He was the only one who was employed. I think he was an accountant. And he was been there for almost 40 years in the same place. And they had a very nice apartment. Lovely for Viennese standards, but they also had to tap outside. And she used to have one of those large things that you water outside flowers with. That was-- she used to bring in the water, and that's what she used all the time. And they lived there for, I don't know, must have lived there for 40 years. In those days, nobody moved. People didn't move. They lived forever in the same place. There was no such thing as saying, I want a nicer place. They lived there. And he used to come-- everybody came home for lunch. Because lunch was not lunch. It was the biggest meal of the day. This is what happened. You ate in the morning a very light-- a very light breakfast, usually a kipferl-- it's like a croissant-- and coffee. And we used to get, of course, milk, which I didn't like, or cocoa. Hot cocoa, hot chocolate. And then at 10:00, you ate a little snack, a sandwich or something, something that was around. And then the big meal was between 12:00

speaker: interviewee
text: 00. And my father always came home for lunch when he had the store. The store was closed. Everything was closed between 12:00 and 2:00. He came home, and he would eat a big meal, and then take a nap. And then go back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, did your father start a new store when they moved back to--

speaker: interviewee
text: He couldn't. He didn't have enough money. And it was very difficult at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did he do at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think he sold from the house. He had some stuff in there. He brought some stuff with him. And it was in another room. I don't know. He sold to people he knew. I don't really know. He didn't tell me what he was doing. I knew he was buying and selling something, and I don't know what it was. It probably was furs because that's what he knew. But I don't know how he did it or what. And the reason he came back because the family always said, we'll help you, we'll help you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Back to Austria?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Vienna, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: To Vienna.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, it wasn't took me long before I hated Vienna. And to this day, I will always say the five years I spent in Vienna were the worst years of my life. I got to hate the town. I got to hate everybody in it. First of all, it wasn't just the makeup and the way people behaved and the way they were talking. I thought they were in the 18th century with their behavior and all of this. It was they weren't nice people. I didn't think they were nice people. They were snobbish. Everybody was a snob and looked down on somebody. You always found somebody to which you thought you were better. Everybody was snobbish. And not only this, they thought of themselves as Viennese, the most educated, the most cultural people in the whole world. Nobody could possibly be better than a Viennese. I mean, there was no such thing. That was the highest.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I didn't think so. So what good is it if you know all the operas if you didn't-- couldn't behave in a decent way to somebody else, to me? I've read all these books, you see. And I had a different idea of what people should or should not be like. And they were all snobs.

speaker: interviewee
text: To this day, I don't have any Viennese friends. I can't have Viennese friends, because they're still the same. They are still snobs. They still look down on everybody. And not only this, they tried to tell everybody what they should do. Everybody knew everything better. Whatever you did, they knew it better. Whatever you had to do, they knew it better.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was glad that we moved out of Artur's house because it was getting very tensions. I mean, Kurt, my cousin, was very nice to me, but he and his mother didn't get along. And they were always shouting at each other. And she was always yelling at him, tried to tell him what to do, and he didn't listen to her. To him, she didn't exist, except to say hello and goodbye. And that's all.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she was hurt, of course. It was her son, her only son, that she had. Her only child. She tried. I must say, she tried, but she couldn't. And I don't know, so that's why I tried to find out what the hell happened between her and her husband. And I remember she tried very hard also to be a housewife. Mitzi did all of the cleaning. And then, after that, she would go around with the broom and do-- and try to make believe that she was looking out for the place. And even in the dentist's place. But he never said anything to her. I think she would have preferred if her husband-- her ex-husband-- would have yelled at her. But he didn't yell at her. He didn't talk to her. And I distinctly remember, one time, it was either his birthday or was a holiday, she came over and she put her arms around him, and she wished him a happy birthday. He very gently, very gently, took her arms off and walked out. Didn't say one word. He didn't say, don't do this, nothing. She would have preferred if he would have said, stop this, or leave me alone. He never said anything to her. And I think that was worse to her than anything else. But she had her boyfriend. She had a man friend. I know she had a-- she used to get dressed and go out. And I don't know what she did. She never told me, nobody. I tried to find out from Kurt. I tried to find out from Mitzi. Mitzi knew, but Mitzi wouldn't tell me. She was very loyal to the family. She said, that's not your business. This is a private affair, and you are not supposed to know. She wouldn't tell me. And I never found out. And I was dying to know what the hell happened. I mean, in those days, people didn't get divorced.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go on with your life in Vienna.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: You lived there for?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived-- from this one, this was a nice apartment, and we went to a cheaper apartment. We went down, down, down, down. And school was horrible for me. And I couldn't wait to get out. Except that I didn't have many friends, so I was very lonely.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went back to what I knew best. I read from morning to night. I read. I lived in the library, and I just read. And to me, life was not there. It was in the books someplace else.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a swimming pool that you could go to in Vienna. It's the Amalienbad, I remember. And they had artificial waves. Every half hour, they would put artificial waves, and it was very fun. I went there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else did I do? Oh, yeah, I think my cousin gave me his old bicycle, and I rode a little bit of a bicycle. But that's all I did. Mainly, I read. I did nothing. Did you see a rise of anti-Semitism during this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: It didn't have to rise. It was there. When I came back, as I said, I was extremely aware that I was Jewish. And I was never aware of that before. Until Hitler came. And there, in Vienna, it was so obvious, the anti-Semitism, that then I understood why my father left. You could not go anywhere without, you know, everybody know you were Jewish. And you were second class or they made remarks. This was all over. It was just-- they were infested with it, you know, like a sickness. And I could understand why they left. And I could understand why my father didn't want to go back. Excuse me. I knew why he didn't want to go back. He was right. But even though, whenever you said something, they would say, oh so-and-so this person is Jewish in the movie industry or on the stage, or professor so-and-so in the sciences is Jewish. And, of course, there was Sigmund Freud, which he wasn't very popular there, though. His psychology did not take hold there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I want to tell, you know, that after Hitler came to Austria, he didn't want to leave. He thought nothing would happen to him. He was Sigmund Freud. Who would do anything to him? They forced him to-- they actually forced him to leave.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the-- when my father told everybody, look, you better get ready to leave, because Hitler is going to come here. They laughed at him. They said, that's ridiculous. In this town, we've lived here generations and generations. And half the population is Jewish. You know, there were a lot of Jews there. There must have been 150,000 or something like that, I think. There are now 10,000, I understand. A few came back. I wouldn't go back. I would never live there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me the story of what happened to your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, so we moved from one apartment to another. Then, finally, I think he borrowed some money from the relatives, and he opened a little grocery store in a different district. And we moved to an apartment right near there, because, in the back, there was a little room where my mother cooked and we lived there practically. We cooked and we ate there because the apartment we had was absolutely the worst. Horrible. It was, I think, two or three rooms. My brother-in-law and I had to share a bedroom. We had no water. We had no toilet. We had to go down one flight of stairs to find a toilet and to get water. And it was just the most primitive thing I have ever seen when we lived in the back of the store. But I hated it. And I had to-- I was-- after school, I was supposed to help out in the store. I didn't like that, either. Mainly, they sent me out if people called up, you know, for-- to get the stuff to the people that called for food.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you know what I did? I remember. I would bring the food in and run to the park to see whether there was anybody there I could talk to. Because I had nobody to talk to. My mother didn't talk to me. My grandmother didn't talk to me. My brother and I, at this point, we were not very close. We were four years apart, and he had his own problems.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had nobody. I had no friends. So I was looking for somebody to talk to. And of course, I was getting to be-- I blossomed out. I got breasts, and my mother bought me a brassiere. She said, you have to wear that from now on. I hated it. [LAUGHING] And so I used to run to the park and then come back late. And my father finally cut it out. He says, you're not to go to the park. I don't know who you're meeting there, but you know the rules. You cannot meet somebody I didn't meet and something like that. You cannot go to the park. Period. That was my life. I think when I was 15 1/2, they let me graduate from school-- barely 16. Because I knew everything, anyway. There was nothing they could teach me. And I wanted to go to the gymnasium, and my mother said there's no money. So then, she said, well, maybe we could find a job for you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my Uncle Artur hired me to write his letters for him on a part-time basis. He knew we needed the money, so that was instead of giving the money, I went there every morning, and I came home for lunch, of course. Everybody came home for the midday meal and went back. So my mother gave me some money to go on the wonderful public transportation. But if I wanted some money for myself, I used to walk. It was quite far the distance. So I could use the money for buying myself a secret lipstick. I had a little powder and stuff like that, you know, which I wasn't allowed to use at home, anyway.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I still went to the theater. That was a given. We used to go and stand, standing room, and to the opera. And that was always there. It was very cheap. We used to go as students. And we used to stand. And that was the only thing I had, really. And sometimes my cousin would go with me. And my father constantly brought all kinds of material, from South America, from China, where we could go. And everybody kept on saying, will you please stop that. You're not going anyplace. Nothing's going to happen here. It's not going to happen here. Not only this, when we told them about what happened, about people were sent to concentration camps, they didn't believe it. The Jews didn't believe it. And they said, well, are you sure he wasn't belong to the Socialist party or the Communist party or some party or union or something. No. They just took people anywhere. If a neighbor was jealous of your furniture, he would go to the authorities and say, you know this man, he made a joke about Hitler. They would come and take him to a concentration camp and took all of his stuff. That's the way it went. It was really horrible. And we heard all kinds of horrible stories of people being beaten up in Germany, and concentration camps were something new. And everybody thought, well, in order to go in the concentration camp, you must have done something. You must have belonged secretly to some party that they didn't approve of. You could have belonged to a union if you were a working men. But my father didn't belong to anything. He didn't even belong to a business club. Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened to your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we went from-- well, we had this little grocery store. But after that lasted about a year, and I hated where we lived. Absolutely hated it. I think I walked around dirty because there was no place where we wash or anything. I remember that. I really think I walked around dirty. Because it was just so hard to do anything. And the apartment was horrible. And my mother had sold all of her beautiful stuff. That wasn't there anymore. And the relatives, I mean, we could-- when we visited relatives, they would give us cookies or something. Only my Uncle Julius, the one that was also a dentist, he would sometimes give us a little money. But all the others, they just gave us a little cookies or cake or something. They didn't do anything for us. And we couldn't talk to them. To me, they were like ancient, in their 60s. I was a child. And that was the life in Vienna. I hated it. I couldn't wait till my father found some place where we could go. Then, in 1937, he talked to somebody, and he found out there was another brother my grandmother had, who was like the black sheep of the family. And he came to the United States at the turn of the century, maybe 1903 or 1905 or maybe 1900. I don't know. But he left Austria in that days, and he came to the United States. In those days, in the early 1900s, if somebody did something in the family, they used to send them to United States. That was the thing to do. The bad people used to go to the United States. They did something, so there, this is where you put all of the rejects.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they must have sent him there. But we found out he had a wife and he had-- that he was also-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and he had three sons. And he found out he had-- well, we'll to go to the United States, absolutely. So we immediately wrote to him and asked him, what do we have to do to come to United States. And then we found out you had to have an affidavit. In other words, you couldn't be going on welfare, come here and go on welfare. You couldn't ask anybody for help. You had to have somebody who would say, I will bring the person over, and I will be responsible to take care of them. So your father wrote to him--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And asked if your family could go to the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. What we had to do. I wrote the letters. My father was not a very good correspondent. He used to dictate to me, and I used to write the letters.

speaker: interviewer
text: So and, to our surprise, and everybody said, oh, that man, he's not going to do anything for anybody. He was badly talked about. I don't know why. To this day, I don't know what happened. That was two generations beyond me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody had-- they found out-- he had to wriggle out-- apparently, somebody knew where he lived. He lived in Brooklyn. But till he found out the address, that was another thing to do. Apparently, only one or two people knew where he lived. And apparently, they didn't write to each other, but they did know where he lived. They kept track of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we wrote to him, and he explained to us that we needed an affidavit. So we asked him could he send us an affidavit. So he says, no, he couldn't, because he was semi-retired.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know what he was doing. He didn't tell us what he was doing. But he was at least 68 or 69. I don't know how old he was. And that he didn't have any money, but he was going to find somebody. He was going to find somebody that would give us an affidavit.
